# **Comprehensive Analysis of the General Transit Feed Specification Realtime (GTFS-RT) and Occupancy Data Integration within the London Transit Commission**

## **1\. Introduction to Real-Time Transit Data and Capacity Tracking**

The evolution of public transportation systems has been fundamentally intertwined with the advancement of open data standards, shifting the paradigm from static scheduling to dynamic, real-time passenger information systems. The General Transit Feed Specification (GTFS), originally developed to standardize static scheduling data, has been exponentially enhanced by the introduction of GTFS-Realtime (GTFS-RT).1 This extension allows transit agencies to transmit live updates regarding vehicle positions, trip delays, and service alerts, creating a cohesive ecosystem where software developers, third-party applications, and urban planners can ingest and interpret transit conditions instantaneously.3  
A critical frontier in this domain is the tracking and broadcasting of vehicle passenger capacity. As urban transit networks face fluctuating ridership demands, demographic shifts, and infrastructural bottlenecks, the ability to monitor and communicate vehicle crowding in real-time has transitioned from a supplementary feature to an operational necessity. For application developers, particularly those designing features such as bus capacity meters, the reliance on algorithmic estimation is increasingly being replaced by the ingestion of deterministic data directly from transit agency feeds. Predictive models, which often rely on historical averages and time-of-day heuristics, frequently fail to account for spontaneous surges in ridership, localized weather events, or cascading schedule delays. Consequently, the GTFS-RT specification has evolved to accommodate this need through dedicated enumerators and fields specifically designed to convey empirical passenger occupancy directly from the vehicle's onboard sensors.3  
This comprehensive analysis provides an exhaustive examination of the GTFS-RT specification with a specialized focus on vehicle occupancy data structures, namely the OccupancyStatus and OccupancyPercentage fields.3 Furthermore, it contextualizes this technical framework within the operational realities of the London Transit Commission (LTC) in London, Ontario, Canada. By analyzing LTC's open data infrastructure, hardware deployments, route topology, and fleet composition, this report aims to determine the availability, reliability, and integration pathways for empirical bus capacity data. The primary objective is to equip application developers with the necessary technical intelligence to eliminate the reliance on predictive capacity models and to diagnose and resolve critical software anomalies, such as the pervasive issue of applications erroneously reporting empty buses due to data deserialization errors.6

## **2\. The Architecture and Mechanics of the GTFS-Realtime Specification**

### **2.1 Origins, Stewardship, and Foundational Principles**

The GTFS-Realtime standard was initially developed through a collaborative partnership involving Google, initial Live Transit Updates partner agencies, and various transit software developers.1 Designed around the principles of implementation simplicity, interoperability with static GTFS data, and a steadfast focus on passenger information, the specification is published under the permissive Apache 2.0 License, fostering widespread global adoption.1 Since early 2019, the stewardship and maintenance of both the static GTFS and dynamic GTFS-RT standards have been managed by MobilityData, an industry consortium dedicated to solving mobility data challenges through standard-setting and the provision of validation tools.9  
MobilityData facilitates community-driven changes, ensuring that the specification adapts to emerging transit technologies and hardware capabilities. Through open voting processes and rigorous community discussions on platforms such as GitHub and dedicated mailing lists, the specification continuously incorporates new fields—ranging from text-to-speech descriptions for service alerts to experimental fields for vehicle crowding and accessibility.10 This collaborative governance ensures that the data structures remain relevant to the evolving operational realities of modern transit agencies.

### **2.2 Protocol Buffers: The Serialization Engine**

The underlying data exchange format for GTFS-RT is Protocol Buffers (frequently abbreviated as Protobuf), a language-neutral, platform-neutral, and highly extensible mechanism for serializing structured data developed by Google.1 Compared to traditional textual formats such as XML or JSON, Protocol Buffers offer a highly compact binary representation that is explicitly optimized for fast, efficient network transmission and low-latency processing on both the server and client sides.1  
The hierarchical data schema governing this exchange is strictly defined in a foundational file named gtfs-realtime.proto.1 To interact with GTFS-RT streams, developers utilize this schema file in conjunction with the protoc compiler to generate language-specific data access classes (bindings) in programming languages such as Java, C++, Python, Golang, and JavaScript/TypeScript.9 These generated classes provide robust APIs for the construction, serialization, and parsing of binary data streams into native data model objects. This architecture ensures type safety and prevents schema-invalid message generation, as the generated code enforces the structural integrity defined in the .proto file.9  
Because Protocol Buffers operate on a strictly typed schema, properties such as enumerators (enums) are tightly controlled. For example, a status field defined as an enum in the schema becomes a native enum construct in the target programming language, ensuring that developers map values cleanly without encountering the ambiguities and performance overhead associated with string parsing.12 This rigid typing system, while highly performant, introduces specific nuances in how missing or null data is handled—a critical factor when diagnosing capacity meter anomalies.

### **2.3 The Tripartite Feed Structure**

A standard GTFS-RT feed is inextricably linked to an existing static GTFS feed and is typically acquired via an HTTP GET request.3 The real-time stream cannot exist in a vacuum; all entity identifiers (such as trip\_id, route\_id, and stop\_id) transmitted in the real-time payload must resolve against the primary keys established in the static GTFS dataset.3 The specification encompasses three distinct, conditionally required feed entities, each serving a unique function in the passenger information ecosystem.

| Feed Entity Type | Primary Function | Key Data Fields | Relationship to Static GTFS |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Trip Updates** (TripUpdate) | Communicates dynamic alterations to the scheduled timetable. | Delays, early arrivals, cancellations, real-time route deviations, StopTimeUpdate. | Modifies trips.txt and stop\_times.txt by providing real-time offsets to scheduled arrival/departure times. |
| **Vehicle Positions** (VehiclePosition) | Broadcasts the spatial and operational status of a specific transit vehicle. | Latitude, longitude, bearing, speed, CongestionLevel, OccupancyStatus, OccupancyPercentage. | Links a physical vehicle to a specific trip\_id and tracks its progression along the geometric path defined in shapes.txt. |
| **Service Alerts** (Alert) | Transmits higher-level qualitative information regarding system disruptions. | Text descriptions, active periods, URL links, severity levels, effect types. | Can be applied network-wide or mapped to specific entities in routes.txt, stops.txt, or trips.txt. |

The modularity of this system allows transit agencies to implement GTFS-RT incrementally. An agency might initially deploy only Service Alerts, subsequently integrating Vehicle Positions as their GPS infrastructure matures, and finally deploying Trip Updates once their predictive arrival algorithms are sufficiently calibrated. For the purpose of tracking passenger crowding, the VehiclePositions feed serves as the primary conduit for telemetry, although the specification theoretically permits occupancy data to be projected into the future via the StopTimeUpdate message within the TripUpdates feed.1

![][image1]

## **3\. Deep Dive: Occupancy Data Specifications in GTFS-RT**

The transmission of vehicle crowding data has undergone rigorous evolution and intense debate within the GTFS-RT standards community. As urban transit systems increasingly prioritize passenger comfort, equitable service distribution, and dynamic loading during peak operational hours, accurately relaying the state of passenger occupancy has become an absolute necessity.7 GTFS-RT facilitates this data exchange through two fields nested within the VehiclePosition and StopTimeUpdate messages: OccupancyStatus and OccupancyPercentage.3

### **3.1 The OccupancyStatus Enumerator**

Introduced into the GTFS-RT specification incrementally, the OccupancyStatus field is a discrete enumerator designed to convey the qualitative degree of passenger occupancy on a given vehicle or carriage.3 Because raw passenger count numbers vary vastly in meaning depending on the physical dimensions of the vehicle, the OccupancyStatus enum serves to translate raw telemetry into a normalized, passenger-facing semantic scale. For instance, an absolute count of 40 passengers signifies that a standard 40-foot bus is nearing its absolute capacity, whereas the exact same count on a 60-foot articulated bus indicates that ample seating remains available.7 The enumerator abstracts this mathematical variance, providing developers with a ready-to-use status classification.

| Integer Value | Enumerator Name | Semantic Description and Application Context |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 0 | EMPTY | Used to indicate there are no (or very few) passengers on board. The vehicle is accepting passengers. Historically, this state generated debate regarding whether it implied a deadhead vehicle or simply zero riders.3 |
| 1 | MANY\_SEATS\_AVAILABLE | The vehicle has a large number of seats available. The specific threshold for what constitutes "many" versus "few" is determined by the agency's internal algorithms.3 |
| 2 | FEW\_SEATS\_AVAILABLE | The vehicle has some seats available and is still accepting passengers. Boarding passengers may need to search for a seat.13 |
| 3 | STANDING\_ROOM\_ONLY | The vehicle has exhausted its seating capacity and can currently accommodate only standing passengers.13 |
| 4 | CRUSHED\_STANDING\_ROOM\_ONLY | The vehicle can accommodate standing passengers but possesses extremely limited physical space. Indicates a severely crowded environment typical of peak urban corridors.13 |
| 5 | FULL | The vehicle is considered full by most operational measures but may theoretically still allow sporadic boarding if passengers compress further.13 |
| 6 | NOT\_ACCEPTING\_PASSENGERS | The vehicle is strictly not accepting additional passengers due to absolute capacity constraints or operational directives.13 |

Consumers of this data must remain cognizant that individual transit agencies, acting as data producers, may not publish the full spectrum of these values.3 The official specification explicitly warns that the OccupancyStatus values do not constitute a strictly linear mathematical scale, and consumers must represent the values in their user interfaces precisely as the qualitative state indicated by the producer.3 If an agency's backend hardware is only configured to output MANY\_SEATS\_AVAILABLE, STANDING\_ROOM\_ONLY, and FULL, a third-party application must be capable of rendering this truncated spectrum without generating interpolation errors.

![][image2]

### **3.2 The OccupancyPercentage Field**

While OccupancyStatus provides excellent semantic categorization, it inherently lacks the granularity required by developers who wish to render precise visual progress bars. To address this limitation, the OccupancyPercentage field was introduced and heavily debated before its adoption to allow producers to transmit crowding on a strict linear scale.5  
Represented as a standard int32 integer, this field transmits a value typically ranging from 0 to 100, though it can technically exceed 100 if extreme crush loads surpass the vehicle's nominal design capacity.3 The introduction of OccupancyPercentage resolved numerous ambiguities for consumers, granting them the mathematical data required to render bespoke capacity meters. However, profound theoretical debates have surfaced regarding the concurrent transmission of both the enum and the percentage fields. Ambiguities arise if a producer transmits an occupancy\_percentage of 90% alongside an occupancy\_status of FULL, prompting ongoing discussions on whether consumers should strictly map the two fields, prioritize one over the other, or if the specification should ultimately deprecate the older enum.5 Currently, the GTFS-RT standard permits the transmission of both fields simultaneously, granting producers maximum flexibility.5

### **3.3 Handling Missing Data and Deserialization Anomalies**

A foundational aspect of consuming the VehiclePositions feed involves the rigorous handling of null states. Due to the realities of transit operations—including hardware sensor failures, localized cellular network latency, or backend server reboots—transit agencies frequently encounter moments where real-time occupancy cannot be verified or transmitted. The prevailing, documented best practice for producers of GTFS-RT is to simply omit the occupancy\_percentage and occupancy\_status fields entirely from the payload when data is unavailable.16  
Because Protocol Buffers handle absent optional fields by applying default values during the parsing phase, consumers must actively check for the presence of the data using library-specific methodologies prior to reading the variable. In environments like Java or C++, this requires invoking methods such as hasOccupancyStatus() or utilizing equivalent has\_field checks in Python.16 If an enum field is completely unpopulated in the binary stream, a robust application architecture must catch this omission and revert the user interface to an "Unknown Capacity" state. Failure to perform this check results in the parser defaulting the enum to its zero-index value. In the OccupancyStatus enum, the zero index correlates directly to the EMPTY state, thereby generating the exact anomaly of falsely broadcasting empty buses to commuters when, in reality, the data is simply missing.3

## **4\. Mechanisms of Data Acquisition: Hardware and Software Synergies**

For GTFS-RT occupancy fields to function, physical transit vehicles must be equipped with specialized hardware capable of empirically measuring passenger loads. This telemetry chain spans from onboard sensors to centralized dispatch servers.

### **4.1 Automatic Passenger Counters (APCs)**

Automatic Passenger Counters (APCs) form the foundational hardware layer for capacity measurement. Historically, transit agencies, including early pilot programs in London, relied on pressure-sensitive treadle mats installed directly onto the vehicle steps.17 While revolutionary at the time, these mechanical systems were prone to physical degradation and struggled to differentiate between passengers and heavy luggage.  
Modern transit fleets universally utilize advanced optical sensors, specifically overhead infrared beam breakers or sophisticated 3D stereoscopic cameras positioned above all boarding and alighting doors.19 As passengers cross the sensor thresholds, the system tallies boardings and alightings in real-time. The onboard central processing unit continuously calculates the net passenger load by subtracting total alightings from total boardings. To maintain accuracy, these systems apply proprietary error-correction algorithms designed to account for sensor occlusion, passengers loitering in the doorway, or individuals carrying large objects.18

### **4.2 CAD/AVL Integration and Telemetry**

The raw load telemetry generated by the APCs is fed directly into the vehicle's onboard router and the Computer-Aided Dispatch and Automatic Vehicle Location (CAD/AVL) system.19 In the North American market, sophisticated vendors such as Clever Devices, INIT, and TripSpark dominate the CAD/AVL landscape, providing the critical bridge between onboard hardware and backend servers.20  
The CAD/AVL system continuously aggregates highly precise location data via GPS, schedule adherence parameters, engine diagnostics, and APC net loads into a comprehensive payload referred to as the "State File".22 This State File records an exhaustive list of parameters including date, time, location, bus route, direction, occupancy status, odometer readings, and current speed.22  
This State File is subsequently transmitted via secure cellular networks to the transit agency's central backend server. It is at this central server level that the proprietary CAD/AVL software stack serializes the telemetry into the standardized Protocol Buffer format defined by the GTFS-RT specification.22 Notably, the configuration of the CAD/AVL backend heavily dictates the quality and structure of the GTFS-RT output. In some industry pilot implementations, agencies utilizing hardware from vendors like Clever Devices discovered that the default vehicle State Files were not natively configured to expose real-time occupancy parameters without specific software modifications or vendor intervention.22 Ensuring that the CAD/AVL provider is actively populating the specific GTFS-RT fields is a prerequisite for any open data initiative.

## **5\. The London Transit Commission (LTC): Operational Context**

To effectively troubleshoot the implementation of capacity meters for the London Transit Commission, it is essential to analyze the agency's specific operational parameters, network topology, and technological stack. The physical characteristics of the fleet dictate how raw passenger counts translate into occupancy percentages.

### **5.1 Fleet Composition and Baseline Capacity**

The London Transit Commission operates a diverse, modern fleet predominantly manufactured by industry leaders such as New Flyer and Nova Bus. The conventional service is operated across 37 fixed routes and 6 community routes.24 The fleet is categorized into three distinct operational classes, each possessing unique capacity constraints that directly influence the OccupancyPercentage calculations.7

| Vehicle Class | Primary Assignment | Seating Capacity | Total Peak Capacity Baseline |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **60-foot Articulated Bus** | High-volume arterial routes and academic corridors. | 56 passenger seats. | Significantly higher than standard buses; possesses massive standing room.7 |
| **40-foot Standard Bus** | The primary workhorse of the conventional transit fleet. | Between 34 and 40 passenger seats. | Baseline capacity of 55 total passengers (seated and standing) is often applied for peak period evaluation.7 |
| **30-foot Community Bus** | Localized neighborhood and community service routes. | 30 passenger seats. | Lower standing capacity; optimized for maneuverability in residential zones.7 |

Calculating an accurate OccupancyPercentage requires the backend CAD/AVL system to dynamically identify the specific vehicle asset ID assigned to a trip, retrieve its physical capacity from a static database, and divide the current APC net load by that capacity. A net load of 40 passengers equals approximately 72% capacity on a 40-foot bus, triggering a FEW\_SEATS\_AVAILABLE or STANDING\_ROOM\_ONLY status, whereas the same net load on a 60-foot bus constitutes a significantly lower percentage and a MANY\_SEATS\_AVAILABLE status.

### **5.2 The Crowding Imperative in the London Network**

Occupancy tracking is not merely a supplementary aesthetic feature for the London Transit Commission; it represents a critical operational parameter addressing widespread customer dissatisfaction. LTC's network design is heavily oriented around a "Primary Transit Area" that connects Central London with high-volume Transit Villages. These transit anchors include Western University, Fanshawe College, and major retail and transfer hubs such as Masonville Mall and White Oaks Mall.25  
Ridership demands in London, exacerbated by a rapidly growing population and demographic shifts, frequently exceed the infrastructural capacity of the transit network. This leads to severe vehicle crowding, which in turn causes cascading schedule adherence degradation as dwell times at stops increase.25 Routes serving the intense academic corridors—such as Route 10, the newly introduced Route 127 along Sarnia Road, and Route 31—frequently experience scenarios where buses must bypass waiting passengers due to maximum crush loads, an operational failure referred to as "overloads".25  
The 2023 Voice of the Customer survey starkly highlighted these operational challenges. Seating and space availability on the bus, along with on-time performance, ranked among the highest priorities for LTC riders. However, these same metrics commanded remarkably low satisfaction ratings, with overall service satisfaction suffering as a result of crowding and delays.30 Consequently, providing transparent, real-time bus capacity data to riders prior to boarding is viewed as a flagship initiative to mitigate customer frustration, empowering users to make informed decisions regarding whether to wait for a subsequent, less crowded vehicle.32

### **5.3 LTC Technological Infrastructure and Vendor Partnerships**

Recognizing the necessity of data-driven management, LTC has invested heavily in modernizing its transit technology suite. The agency utilizes a comprehensive CAD/AVL system provided by Clever Devices, a premier vendor in the North American transit technology sector.19 This robust backend aggregates telemetry from the onboard APC systems.7  
Crucially, the Clever Devices platform is fully capable of outputting GTFS-RT compliant feeds. Recent industry developments have seen Clever Devices natively integrate their GTFS-RT outputs with major third-party applications like Transit App. As LTC currently recommends Transit App as a primary trip planning interface, ensuring the integrity and completeness of the GTFS-RT feed output from the Clever Devices backend is a foundational requirement for delivering accurate crowding information to the public.23

## **6\. Analyzing the LTC Open Data Feed and Implementation Validation**

The London Transit Commission actively promotes open data access, granting developers a worldwide, royalty-free, perpetual, and non-exclusive license to utilize, modify, and distribute its datasets for commercial and non-commercial purposes.6 This robust open data policy provides the legal and technical foundation for any application seeking to display accurate bus capacity.

### **6.1 Feed Endpoints, Formats, and Infrastructure**

LTC exposes its real-time data through two concurrent formats: Protocol Buffers (.pb) for low-latency, high-performance production applications, and JSON (.json) for debugging, rapid prototyping, and lightweight web parsing.6

| GTFS-RT Entity | Protocol Buffer Endpoint | JSON Endpoint |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Vehicle Positions** | http://gtfs.ltconline.ca/Vehicle/VehiclePositions.pb | http://gtfs.ltconline.ca/Vehicle/VehiclePositions.json |
| **Trip Updates** | http://gtfs.ltconline.ca/TripUpdate/TripUpdates.pb | http://gtfs.ltconline.ca/TripUpdate/TripUpdates.json |
| **Alerts** | http://gtfs.ltconline.ca/Alert/Alerts.pb | http://gtfs.ltconline.ca/Alert/Alerts.json |

The VehiclePositions feed is the focal point for capacity data. Given the dynamic nature of transit operations, the feed is updated with high frequency. Community-developed Python scripts and open-source projects designed to interact with the LTC network (such as those archived on GitHub) establish standard polling intervals at approximately 10 seconds to maintain real-time positional and capacity accuracy (REFRESH\_LTC\_SECONDS \= 10).39

### **6.2 Empirical Evidence of Capacity Transmission**

Before a developer expends resources attempting to extract capacity data from the VehiclePositions feed, it is imperative to verify that the transit agency is actively populating those specific fields. To determine whether LTC is natively transmitting capacity data, one must cross-reference the agency's proprietary consumer-facing tools with their open-data output.  
LTC operates a proprietary web-based tracking portal (realtime.londontransit.ca). Documentation for this portal explicitly instructs users on how to check bus capacity, stating that the interface displays details on the bus including the "percentage of capacity," alongside on-time performance, previous stop location, and direction.32  
The presence of a highly functional, percentage-based capacity meter on LTC’s own web infrastructure provides definitive confirmation that the backend Clever Devices system is successfully processing raw APC data, calculating net loads against specific fleet baselines, and serving it externally.7 Therefore, third-party developers do not need to rely on predictive machine learning models or historical estimations for LTC buses; the deterministic data is being generated, serialized, and transmitted.

## **7\. Diagnosing and Resolving the "Empty Bus" Anomaly**

If a third-party application relies on the GTFS-RT specification but consistently registers functional, revenue-generating LTC buses as "Empty," the issue is highly unlikely to be a failure of the physical APC hardware or the Clever Devices backend. Instead, the anomaly stems from a profound mismatch in how the application's software parses the VehiclePositions.pb binary feed relative to how the Protocol Buffer specification handles missing data.  
Given the architecture of the GTFS-RT specification, there are three primary hypotheses for this specific malfunction:

### **7.1 Hypothesis 1: Default Value Fallbacks in Deserialization**

This is the most probable cause of the "empty bus" bug. When an LTC vehicle's APC system is temporarily offline, or if the cellular connection drops, the backend server should ideally omit both the occupancy\_percentage and occupancy\_status fields entirely from the generated payload.16  
However, when a naive parsing library (particularly in dynamically typed languages like Python or JavaScript) deserializes a Protocol Buffer message that is missing an enum field, it frequently assigns the default value of 0 to that variable. In the context of the OccupancyStatus enum, the integer 0 correlates directly and exclusively to the EMPTY state.13 Therefore, a genuine lack of data is falsely rendered to the user as a completely empty bus. Developers must proactively utilize library-specific presence checks, such as hasOccupancyStatus() in Java or the HasField() method in Python, prior to attempting to read the value.16

### **7.2 Hypothesis 2: Strict Reliance on OccupancyStatus vs. OccupancyPercentage**

LTC's native web tools explicitly and prominently display the "percentage of capacity".32 It is highly probable that the Clever Devices backend is prioritized to populate the occupancy\_percentage integer field rather than mapping the data to the seven-tiered qualitative OccupancyStatus enum. If a third-party application is strictly polling the feed for the OccupancyStatus enumerator and ignoring the OccupancyPercentage field entirely, it will fail to read the capacity. If the parser then defaults the missing enum to 0, the UI will perpetually display an empty bus.

### **7.3 Hypothesis 3: JSON vs. Protobuf Type Conversion Errors**

Developers utilizing the VehiclePositions.json endpoint for prototyping must recognize that Protobuf-to-JSON serialization tools handle enums inconsistently. Depending on the server-side configuration, the JSON output might render the enum as its raw integer (e.g., 0 through 6\) or as its string representation (e.g., "MANY\_SEATS\_AVAILABLE").12 If the client application strictly expects an integer but receives a string (or vice versa), the capacity parsing logic will silently throw an exception. A poorly designed error-handling block might catch this exception and default the UI to a zero/empty state to prevent the application from crashing.

![][image3]

## **8\. Strategic Recommendations for Application Developers**

To construct a resilient and highly accurate capacity tracking feature for the London transit network, developers must pivot entirely away from predictive algorithms and embrace a highly defensive software architecture when parsing the GTFS-RT VehiclePositions feed. The following protocol outlines the optimized integration pathway to eliminate the "empty bus" bug and maximize data fidelity.

### **8.1 Prioritize the OccupancyPercentage Field**

Because LTC’s native tracking solution (realtime.londontransit.ca) is explicitly engineered to output capacity percentages, third-party developers should prioritize parsing the occupancy\_percentage integer field over the semantic OccupancyStatus enum.32 This approach avoids relying on the arbitrary, producer-defined mathematical thresholds that dictate the difference between MANY\_SEATS\_AVAILABLE versus FEW\_SEATS\_AVAILABLE.3 By ingesting the raw percentage, the developer retains complete control over the user interface rendering, enabling the creation of custom gradients or dynamic progress bars that align seamlessly with the specific application's design language.

### **8.2 Implement Strict Null Checking Protocols**

The primary catalyst for "broken" capacity meters is the mishandling of null states. Because transit hardware operates in highly dynamic, physically abusive environments, APCs will invariably experience intermittent network dropouts, sensor occlusion, or recalibration phases. When this occurs, the Clever Devices backend will generate a VehiclePosition message lacking occupancy fields entirely. Applications must employ strict has\_field checks within their Protocol Buffer parsing routines.16 If an occupancy field is unpopulated, the UI must definitively switch to a neutral "Unknown Capacity" or "Data Unavailable" state, rather than silently defaulting to an index of 0 (EMPTY).

### **8.3 Utilize JSON Exclusively for Visual Diagnostics**

While LTC provides highly accessible .json endpoints (gtfs.ltconline.ca/Vehicle/VehiclePositions.json) that are excellent for rapid initial development, these endpoints should be strictly reserved for visual diagnostics and post-mortem log analysis.6 The JSON payload size is significantly larger than the binary .pb format, exponentially increasing cellular payload demands and battery drain for mobile clients polling the feed. Furthermore, JSON endpoints rely heavily on the serialization logic of the host server, which can lead to frustrating inconsistencies when interpreting nested enum values.12 Production applications querying the LTC open data feed at the standard 10-second polling interval must utilize the .pb endpoint to ensure optimal network latency and mathematically robust type safety.9

### **8.4 Align with System Architecture and Edge Cases**

Understanding the physical limitations and topological pressures of the LTC network provides valuable context for edge-case software handling. For example, during intense peak transit periods—such as academic ingress/egress around the Fanshawe College and Western University corridors—occupancy\_percentage values may briefly exceed 100% due to dense standing room dynamics and crush loads.7  
Applications should cap user interface progress bars gracefully at 100% to prevent rendering errors, while simultaneously utilizing a secondary visual indicator (e.g., a flashing red "CRUSH LOAD" warning icon) if the ingested integer value exceeds 100\. This logic effectively simulates the critical CRUSHED\_STANDING\_ROOM\_ONLY enum state without sacrificing the granularity of the percentage data.13

## **9\. Conclusion**

The integration of empirical passenger capacity data into third-party transit applications represents a critical advancement in urban mobility technology. By eliminating the reliance on flawed predictive capacity models, developers can provide commuters with highly actionable intelligence regarding system crowding, directly influencing travel decisions and mitigating the profound frustrations associated with overloaded corridors.  
The London Transit Commission's extensive utilization of Clever Devices CAD/AVL systems and sophisticated Automatic Passenger Counters establishes a robust hardware foundation for real-time occupancy tracking. An exhaustive analysis of LTC's proprietary tracking portals definitively confirms that capacity metrics are dynamically calculated, serialized, and served outward by the agency. By critically examining the GTFS-Realtime specification—specifically the interplay between the OccupancyStatus enum and the OccupancyPercentage integer field—developers can successfully overcome the common serialization pitfalls that result in false "Empty Bus" readings. Through rigorous adherence to Protocol Buffer parsing rules, strict null-checking protocols, and strategic prioritization of percentage-based data, third-party consumers can seamlessly extract and render LTC's rich capacity telemetry, ultimately providing a superior, data-driven transit experience.

16. No data available for Occupancy data in GTFS-RT \- Google Groups, accessed May 20, 2026, [https://groups.google.com/g/gtfs-realtime/c/m59LIRHHIGQ](https://groups.google.com/g/gtfs-realtime/c/m59LIRHHIGQ)
